name: Mulberry River

calibration.unit: ft (gage height / USGS param 00065; same datum as NWS AHPS gauge MLBA4)
calibration.keySource: Turner Bend Outfitter staff-gauge key (transferred to the USGS datum, validated in the ~1.5-3.6 ft floatable range) + American Whitewater USGS-gauge correlation (high-water levels) + NWS AHPS flood stages (dangerous end, native to this gauge)

too_low.source: Turner Bend key: below ~1.0-1.2 ft = too low / 'kayaks only, wear good shoes' (heavy dragging). Transferred to USGS datum; today's USGS reading 1.66 ft sits just above this. https://www.turnerbend.com/WaterLevel.html

low.source: Turner Bend key: 1.5-1.7 ft = 'getting low for canoes, kayaks still do well' (floatable with some dragging). Cross-check: USGS 1.66 ft today = summer-low but floatable. https://www.turnerbend.com/WaterLevel.html

optimal_min.source: Turner Bend: 1.8-2.2 ft = 'good low-water floating / good flow'; Byrd's: 2-3 ft 'runnable, drag a few spots'. Cross-check: USGS 2.54 ft observed = 'good'. https://www.turnerbend.com/WaterLevel.html , https://byrdsadventurecenter.com/mulberry-river/

optimal_max.source: American Whitewater: prime 'run of fun' 3.0-3.5 ft on the USGS 'near Mulberry' gauge; Turner Bend: 'prime whitewater' 3.1-3.6 ft; Byrd's: 3-4 ft 'sweet spot'. Note: 3+ ft is lively whitewater that 'can swamp canoes'. https://www.americanwhitewater.org/content/River/view/river-detail/103/main

high.source: American Whitewater (explicitly on the USGS gauge, in ft): 6-7 ft = big-water intermediate playspot, 7-8 ft = advanced. Onset of 'high/pushy, experienced only'. Exact onset between optimal (~3.5 ft) and NWS action stage (10 ft) is not precisely documented. https://www.americanwhitewater.org/content/River/view/river-detail/103/main

dangerous.source: American Whitewater: 'over 8 ft = advanced paddlers only' -> casual/rental do-not-float line. Escalates to NWS action stage 10 ft (official high-water) and minor flood 18 ft (HIGH confidence, native to this gauge). https://www.americanwhitewater.org/content/River/view/river-detail/103/main , https://water.noaa.gov/gauges/mlba4

calibration.notes: UNIT REASONING: USGS 07252000 reports both cfs (~283 today) and gage height (1.66 ft today). I chose FEET because every authoritative floatability source (Turner Bend, Byrd's, American Whitewater) and the NWS flood ladder is expressed in feet; the community publishes essentially no cfs floatability thresholds. CRITICAL TWO-GAUGE CAVEAT: the outfitter floatability key (too_low..optimal_max) originates on Turner Bend's OWN staff gauge at their Cass landing, a DIFFERENT physical gauge ~23 river-miles UPSTREAM of USGS 07252000, with a different stage datum (Turner Bend's channel 'floods'/refunds above ~4.5-5 ft; USGS 07252000 doesn't reach flood until 18 ft). The transfer of Turner Bend's ft numbers onto the USGS datum is justified by THREE consistent cross-checks in the ~1.5-3.6 ft range (prime 3.0-3.5 ft AW/USGS = 3.1-3.6 ft Turner Bend; USGS 1.66 ft = summer-low; USGS 2.54 ft = 'good'), but is NOT validated above ~3.6 ft, where the two gauges diverge. HIGH-CONFIDENCE anchors native to USGS 07252000 (NWS AHPS / MLBA4, NAVD88 datum ~433 ft): action stage 10 ft, minor flood 18 ft, moderate flood 20 ft, major flood 22 ft. OBSERVED stage-flow pairs (for rough cfs cross-reference only): 1.67 ft=283 cfs, 1.78 ft=348 cfs, 2.54 ft=830 cfs; all-time peak 41,300 cfs; median ~38 cfs. CLASSIC RULE OF THUMB: floatable roughly 1.5-4 ft (prime 2.5-3.5 ft); below ~1.2 ft you drag (kayaks only); >~4 ft is pushy, >8 ft experts-only; NWS flood at 18 ft. GAPS: high (6 ft) is LOW confidence; a true cfs-keyed floatability ladder could not be sourced. If the app can add a manual/secondary reference, Turner Bend's landing gauge is the single most authoritative floatability source but has no public API/site ID. It is currently summer-low and the river is 'generally not floatable in the hot summer months.'

secondaryGauges.name: Turner Bend landing staff gauge (Cass, AR / Hwy 23)
secondaryGauges.note: The authoritative outfitter floatability gauge, read in 0.2 ft increments at Turner Bend's landing ~23 river-miles UPSTREAM of USGS 07252000. Manual reading only (posted by Turner Bend); NO USGS site ID or public API. Full key (their datum): <1.0 too low; 1.0-1.4 kayaks only; 1.5-1.7 low for canoes; 1.8-2.0 good low-water, too low for rafts; 2.1-2.6 good flow/whitewater (raft OK ~2.5+); 2.7-3.0 challenging/good fun; 3.1-3.6 prime whitewater (swamps canoes); 3.7-4.0 big fast water; 4.1-4.4 skilled boaters only; 4.5-5.0 too high, can't get under the low-water bridge (deposit refunded). Source: https://www.turnerbend.com/WaterLevel.html

metadata.region: Ozark National Forest / Boston Mountains, NW Arkansas (Franklin, Johnson & Crawford Counties)
metadata.difficultyRating: Class I-II (II+ to III at high water)
metadata.description: The Mulberry is Arkansas's premier float and whitewater river: a ~70-mile, free-flowing (undammed), spring- and rain-fed stream that drops out of the Boston Mountains of the Ozark National Forest and runs generally west-southwest to the Arkansas River near the town of Mulberry. It offers scenic Ozark canyon floating with tree-lined bluffs and reliable Class I-II rapids that stiffen to II+/III at higher water. It is notoriously FLASHY - rising and falling several feet within a day after rain - and is typically runnable only from late October to mid-June, often becoming too low to float in the hot summer months. Popular access runs cluster on the upper/middle river along Highway 215 (Wolf Pen, Byrd's, High Bank, Redding) down to Turner Bend at Cass on Highway 23.
metadata.nhdReachHint: Mulberry River NHD flowline: headwaters in the Boston Mountains near Fallsville / Fly Gap (Newton-Franklin County line), flowing generally west then southwest ~70 river miles through the Ozark National Forest past Oark, Wolf Pen, Byrd's, Redding, and Cass/Turner Bend (Hwy 23 crossing) to its mouth at the Arkansas River near the town of Mulberry, Crawford County (SW of Ozark). USGS gauge 07252000 sits on the lower river near Mulberry, a few miles above the mouth.
metadata.managingAuthority: U.S. Forest Service - Ozark-St. Francis National Forest (Boston Mountain & Pleasant Hill Ranger Districts). Also a component of the National Wild & Scenic Rivers System (designated 1992) and an Arkansas Natural & Scenic River / Extraordinary Resource Water (state-designated 1985).
metadata.accessLaw: Arkansas navigable-waters access: the Mulberry is a legally navigable stream, so the public may float, fish, and wade on the water and below the ordinary high-water mark. However, the streambed banks and adjacent land are largely private or National Forest - there is no general right to trespass on private banks. Put in / take out only at established public accesses (USFS recreation areas such as Wolf Pen, Redding, and outfitter landings such as Byrd's and Turner Bend); some private accesses charge a fee.

hazards: Flash flooding: very flashy, undammed river; can rise several feet within hours after rain (all-time USGS peak 41,300 cfs vs ~283 cfs today). Never launch on a rising gauge or with heavy rain forecast upstream.

Low-water bridge(s) near Turner Bend/Redding: 'stay to the right to get under the Low Water Bridge'; becomes an impassable, dangerous strainer at high water (Turner Bend refuses rentals once too high to get under it, ~4.5 ft on their gauge).

Named rapids that stiffen and can swamp/pin boats at higher flows: Whoop and Holler (big-wave playspot at 6-8+ ft on USGS gauge), Hell Roaring Falls, Hamm Falls (Hamm's Fall), Big Al's Twist, Chainsaw Jungle, Jump Start, Troll Shoal, Sacroiliac Rapid.
Strainers / fallen trees, notably in the second mile below Turner Bend; tight curves at high water that can pin or damage boats.
Class jumps to II+/III at high water; upper Wolf Pen-to-Redding reach is Class II+ and Redding-to-Turner Bend can be Class III at high flows - not for beginners when pushy.
Summer low water: below ~1.2 ft (gauge) most riffles must be dragged or portaged; river is often not floatable June-October.
Remote Ozark canyon with limited cell service; cold water hazard early in the season (spring/late fall).
